source.title: Fidelity Variants of RNA Dependent RNA Polymerases Uncover an Indirect, Mutagenic Activity of Amiloride Compounds

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study began with a screen for RNA mutagen resistance in CVB3 to identify high-fidelity RdRp variants. Unexpectedly, the isolated A372V variant was also resistant to amiloride, a compound with no known mutagenic activity. This led to the hypothesis that amiloride has a hidden mutagenic effect. The research then aimed to characterize this mutagenic activity, understand its mechanism, and explore how different RdRp variants resist it. The initial problem was to understand RNA mutagen resistance mechanisms, but the discovery of cross-resistance shifted the focus to uncovering amiloride's indirect mutagenic activity and its link to ion concentration changes.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: In a screen for RNA mutagen resistance, a high-fidelity RdRp variant (A372V) of CVB3 was isolated. This variant was also resistant to amiloride, leading to the hypothesis that amiloride has a mutagenic activity. Amiloride compounds increased mutation frequencies of CVB3 and poliovirus, and high-fidelity variants were more resistant. The mutagenic activity is proposed to be mediated through alterations in intracellular Mg2+ and Mn2+, affecting RdRp fidelity. Another amiloride-resistant variant, S299T, is completely resistant to this mutagenic activity and unaffected by ion changes. Two resistance mechanisms are identified: increased fidelity (A372V) and resistance to divalent cation concentration changes (S299T).
process_signals: Initial screen for RNA mutagen resistance unexpectedly yielded a variant also resistant to amiloride.
Hypothesis generated: amiloride has a previously undescribed mutagenic activity.
Two distinct resistance mechanisms discovered.

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Amiloride is a potassium-sparing diuretic with antiviral properties, known to inhibit viroporins and RNA synthesis. Harrison et al. isolated two CVB3 RdRp mutants resistant to amiloride, but the mechanism was unclear. The authors aimed to identify high-fidelity RdRp variants by screening for resistance to RNA mutagens (ribavirin, 5-azacytidine). A similar screen previously identified a high-fidelity poliovirus variant. The study extends this to CVB3 and discovers that the same mutation conferring mutagen resistance also confers amiloride resistance, leading to the hypothesis of amiloride's mutagenic activity.
process_signals: Background: amiloride known as non-mutagenic, antiviral via RNA synthesis inhibition.
Goal: identify high-fidelity RdRp variants in CVB3.
Unexpected link: mutagen resistance mutation also confers amiloride resistance.
methods: Screen for resistance to RNA mutagens (ribavirin, 5-azacytidine)

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Isolation of RNA mutagen resistant Coxsackie virus (CVB3) with a high fidelity RdRp
section_gists.gist: CVB3 was treated with various concentrations of RNA mutagens to determine conditions for generating resistance. The text is truncated, but the process involved passaging virus in the presence of mutagens and sequencing to identify mutations. The A372V variant emerged.
process_signals: Optimization of mutagen concentrations for selection.
Passaging and sequencing to identify resistance mutations.

section_gists.heading: The dual resistance of A372V to RNA mutagens and to amiloride is not a product of increased replicative capacity
section_gists.gist: A372V was previously isolated in a screen for amiloride resistance. It replicated to higher titers than wild type in amiloride. To test if this was due to increased replicative capacity, one-step growth curves and northern blot analysis were performed. No significant differences in virus production kinetics or RNA synthesis were found between wild type and A372V in the absence of amiloride. In the presence of amiloride, differences were not statistically significant except at one time point, and RNA synthesis was similarly inhibited. Thus, heightened replicative capacity was not responsible for resistance.
process_signals: Observation: A372V is resistant to both mutagens and amiloride.
Hypothesis tested: resistance due to increased replicative capacity.
Result: hypothesis rejected; resistance not due to replicative capacity.

section_gists.heading: Amiloride compounds have a RNA mutagenic activity
section_gists.gist: Since dual resistance was unlikely from two unrelated mechanisms, the authors hypothesized amiloride has a mutagenic activity. They measured mutation frequencies of wild type and A372V CVB3 treated with amiloride or EIPA. Amiloride increased mutation frequency in wild type, but A372V had lower basal and induced mutation frequencies. The text includes figures and tables showing mutation frequency data and experimental design (RT-PCR, cloning, sequencing).
process_signals: Hypothesis: amiloride has a mutagenic activity.
Experiment: measure mutation frequencies under amiloride treatment.
Result: amiloride increases mutation frequency; high-fidelity variant shows lower frequencies.
methods: Mutation frequency measurement via RT-PCR, cloning, and sequencing of a 1.3 kb capsid region

section_gists.heading: Another high fidelity RdRp variant (poliovirus G64S) is also resistant to the mutagenic effect of amiloride
section_gists.gist: The poliovirus G64S high-fidelity variant was tested to see if the mutagenic effect of amiloride is general. G64S had lower basal mutation frequency than wild type poliovirus, and amiloride increased mutation frequency in both, but G64S remained lower. G64S also showed higher survival under amiloride treatment. This confirms that increased fidelity confers resistance to amiloride across different viruses.
process_signals: Test generality: use poliovirus G64S high-fidelity variant.
Result: amiloride mutagenic effect confirmed in poliovirus; high-fidelity variant resistant.

section_gists.heading: Another polymerase variant of CVB3, S299T, resists amiloride mutagenic activity by a different mechanism
section_gists.gist: S299T, another amiloride-resistant variant, was not isolated in mutagen screens. It was resistant to amiloride but did not show higher fidelity; instead, it had a higher basal mutation frequency, suggesting lower fidelity. In vitro biochemical assay confirmed lower fidelity. S299T mutation frequency did not increase with amiloride or EIPA, indicating complete resistance to the mutagenic effect. S299T also showed less inhibition of RNA synthesis by amiloride. Thus, S299T resists both RNA synthesis inhibition and mutagenesis, unlike A372V which only resists mutagenesis via higher fidelity.
process_signals: Hypothesis: S299T resists amiloride by a different mechanism.
Experiment: measure mutation frequency and fidelity of S299T.
Result: S299T is lower fidelity, but mutation frequency unaffected by amiloride; also partially resistant to RNA synthesis inhibition.

section_gists.heading: The mutagenic effect of amiloride is secondary to inhibition of RNA synthesis
section_gists.gist: To assess the relative contribution of mutagenesis vs. RNA synthesis inhibition, mutation frequencies and virus titers were measured at increasing amiloride concentrations. Unlike ribavirin, which showed a dose-dependent increase in mutation frequency correlating with titer decrease, amiloride did not show a dose-dependent increase in mutation frequency above 100 μM, while virus titers decreased dose-dependently. Northern blot confirmed dose-dependent RNA synthesis inhibition. Thus, RNA synthesis inhibition is the principal antiviral effect, and mutagenesis is an indirect, dose-independent effect.
process_signals: Question: what is the relative contribution of mutagenesis vs. RNA synthesis inhibition?
Experiment: dose-response of mutation frequency and titer for ribavirin and amiloride.
Result: amiloride mutagenesis is dose-independent, secondary to RNA synthesis inhibition.

section_gists.heading: Increases in intracellular ion concentrations (Mg2+, Mn2+) may account for the mutagenic activity of amiloride compounds
section_gists.gist: The dose-independent mutagenesis led to the hypothesis of an indirect mechanism via ion concentration changes. Amiloride is known to affect Na+ channels and antiporters, potentially altering Mg2+ and Mn2+ levels. Experiments showed that increasing extracellular Mg2+ or Mn2+ increased CVB3 mutation frequency. A372V was less sensitive, while S299T was unaffected. Passaging wild type virus in high Mg2+/Mn2+ selected for A372V, not S299T. Intracellular Mg2+ measurements confirmed amiloride increases Mg2+ (though high concentration needed). Thus, amiloride-induced ion changes likely increase mutation frequency by affecting RdRp fidelity.
process_signals: Hypothesis: amiloride mutagenesis is indirect, via ion concentration changes.
Experiment: test effect of Mg2+ and Mn2+ on mutation frequency.
Result: increased ions increase mutation frequency; fidelity variants respond differently.
Selection experiment: high ions select for A372V.
Measurement: amiloride increases intracellular Mg2+.
failures_or_limits: High amiloride concentration (800 μM) needed to detect Mg2+ change due to detection limits.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The study identifies a new mutagenic effect of amiloride. RNA synthesis inhibition is the principal antiviral activity, likely via direct RdRp interaction. Mutagenesis is indirect, likely via ion concentration changes (Mg2+, Mn2+). A372V resists mutagenesis by higher fidelity; S299T resists both mutagenesis and RNA synthesis inhibition, possibly due to altered ion dependence. The mutagenic effect is moderate but sufficient to select for resistance. Limitations include unclear relative contributions in vivo, unknown direct targets of amiloride for ion effects, and the need for further biochemical studies.
process_signals: Synthesis: two antiviral activities of amiloride identified.
Mechanism: RNA synthesis inhibition direct; mutagenesis indirect via ions.
Resistance mechanisms: increased fidelity (A372V) vs. ion insensitivity (S299T).
Limitations acknowledged: relative contributions, in vivo relevance, exact ion targets.
failures_or_limits: Mutagenic activity not as strong as nucleoside analogs.
Unclear if mutagenesis is strong enough for lethal mutagenesis or could enhance evolution.
Exact ion transporters affected by amiloride unknown.
Relative contribution of mutagenesis vs. RNA synthesis inhibition not fully quantified in vivo.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: In vitro biochemical assay for measuring RdRp fidelity
section_gists.gist: Describes materials for in vitro fidelity assay: RNA oligonucleotides, radiolabeled ATP, T4 polynucleotide kinase, nucleotides, and reagents. RNA oligonucleotides were purified and end-labeled. Concentrations determined by absorbance.

section_gists.heading: In vitro nucleotide incorporation assays
section_gists.gist: Describes primer extension assays and stopped-flow fluorescence assays to measure nucleotide incorporation by CVB3 RdRp. Conditions include MgCl2, ZnCl2, and specific buffers. Products resolved by PAGE. Stopped-flow used to measure kinetics of incorporation with 2-aminopurine-labeled primer-template.
methods: Primer extension assay with radiolabeled primer

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from a screen for RNA mutagen resistance to the discovery of amiloride's mutagenic activity.
The dual resistance of A372V prompted the hypothesis that amiloride is mutagenic.
The mutagenic effect is indirect, mediated by changes in intracellular Mg2+ and Mn2+.
Different RdRp variants resist amiloride through distinct mechanisms: increased fidelity (A372V) or insensitivity to ion changes (S299T).
RNA synthesis inhibition is the primary antiviral effect, with mutagenesis as a secondary, dose-independent effect.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the exact molecular target of amiloride that leads to ion concentration changes?
What is the relative contribution of mutagenesis vs. RNA synthesis inhibition to the overall antiviral effect in vivo?
Is the mutagenic effect strong enough to achieve lethal mutagenesis over prolonged exposure?
Could the moderate increase in mutation frequency enhance viral evolution or immune escape?
How does S299T achieve resistance to both RNA synthesis inhibition and mutagenesis? Are these mechanisms coupled?
What are the specific ion transporters affected by amiloride in the context of viral infection?

events.summary: The study aimed to identify high-fidelity RdRp variants of CVB3 by screening for resistance to RNA mutagens.
methods_or_tools: screen for resistance to RNA mutagens

events.summary: A C-to-U mutation resulting in A372V emerged in the RdRp after passage in both mutagens, and its role in resistance was confirmed by reverse genetics.
Mutation frequency analysis showed A372V has approximately 2-fold lower mutation frequency than wild type, confirming higher fidelity.
In vitro biochemical assay confirmed A372V RdRp has higher fidelity, showing 2-fold lower GMP misincorporation rate.
The dual resistance of A372V to RNA mutagens and amiloride led to the hypothesis that amiloride has a mutagenic activity.
One-step growth analysis and northern blot showed no significant difference in replicative capacity between wild type and A372V, ruling out increased replication as the cause of dual resistance.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk covers the initial screen, isolation of A372V, confirmation of high fidelity, and the hypothesis linking amiloride to mutagenesis. It includes key experimental events and the problem statement.

events.summary: In vitro biochemical assays confirm higher fidelity of A372V and lower fidelity of S299T RdRp variants.
numbers_or_metrics: relative incorporation product at 2 and 5 minutes

events.summary: Quantification of relative incorporation product at 2 and 5 minutes reaction time for wild type, A372V, and S299T.
numbers_or_metrics: wild type: 0.27 at 2 min, 0.49 at 5 min

events.summary: Determination of incorporation kinetics and relative fidelity of CVB3 RdRp variants using stopped-flow and primer-extension assays.

S299T kobs correct: 7.4±0.2 s-1, incorrect: 0.008±0.001 s-1, fidelity: 925

events.summary: Lower basal mutation frequency of A372V explains better tolerance to amiloride-induced mutagenesis and higher titers.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key experimental results: in vitro fidelity assays, mutation frequency measurements under amiloride and derivatives, and growth kinetics confirming A372V resistance is not due to replicative capacity.

events.summary: Amiloride treatment increased but did not bias the natural mutation profile, unlike ribavirin which biased mutations to GtoA and CtoT transitions.
Hypothesis that S299T resists amiloride by a different mechanism than A372V, possibly resistance to RNA synthesis inhibition or mutagenic activity.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key experimental results and hypotheses about amiloride's mutagenic activity, resistance mechanisms, and indirect ion-mediated mutagenesis.

events.summary: Hypothesized that amiloride mutagenesis is indirect, resulting from changes in intracellular ion concentrations.
Serially passaged wild type virus in high Mg2+ or Mn2+ and sequenced RdRp regions to identify selected mutations.
Concluded that amiloride-induced mutagenesis results from changes in intracellular divalent cation cofactors, supported by similar sensitivity/resistance profiles.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains key experimental evidence linking amiloride mutagenesis to divalent cation changes, including mutation frequency measurements, selection experiments, and intracellular Mg2+ confirmation.
Chunk contains key experimental evidence for the indirect mutagenic mechanism of amiloride via ion changes, including hypothesis, experiments, and limitations.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains supplementary figure descriptions, author contributions, and references. Some limitations from discussion are repeated, but only those explicitly in this chunk are extracted.

The A372V and S299T variants were constructed using the Quikchange XL site directed mutagenesis kit (Stratagene) and the CVB3-Nancy infectious cDNA.

Nevertheless, the mutagenesis and resistance profiles of the A372V and S299T amiloride-resistant variants support this link between cation concentrations and amiloride's mutagenic effect.

The S299T variant seems to resist amiloride on both fronts: RNA synthesis is less inhibited (Figure 5E) and its mutation frequency is unchanged (Figure 5F).
Unlike S299T, this variant is solely resistant to the mutagenic antiviral activity since its RNA synthesis is as inhibited by amiloride as wild type virus (Figure 3E).

Accordingly, A372V mutation frequencies increased following ribavirin treatment, amiloride treatment and treatment with high concentrations of $Mg^{2+}$ and $Mn^{2+}$ but in all cases, the frequencies were significantly lower than wild type populations under the same treatments.
A372V is a higher fidelity RdRp variant that generates virus populations with lower basal mutation frequencies than wild type populations.

As shown in Figure 2, S299T RdRp incorporated more GMP over time than wild type RdRp, consistent with the S299T mutation decreasing RdRp fidelity (Table 1).
To confirm the lower fidelity phenotype conferred by the S299T mutation, an in vitro biochemical assay examining the relative levels of misincorporation with the purified S299T enzyme was performed.

Finally, to examine the potential effects of the S299T mutation on RdRp fidelity, infections were performed in the presence of ribavirin, amiloride, EIPA, or under mock-treatment and mutation frequencies were determined for each population.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Uncover the indirect mutagenic activity of amiloride compounds and understand how different RdRp fidelity variants resist it.

canonical_events.summary: Screen for high-fidelity RdRp variants of CVB3 by selecting for resistance to RNA mutagens ribavirin and 5-azacytidine.
Isolation of A372V RdRp variant after passage in mutagens; confirmed resistance by reverse genetics.
Mutation frequency analysis shows A372V has ~2-fold lower mutation frequency than wild type, confirming higher fidelity.
In vitro biochemical assay confirms A372V RdRp has higher fidelity (2-fold lower GMP misincorporation).
Dual resistance of A372V to mutagens and amiloride leads to hypothesis that amiloride has mutagenic activity.
One-step growth and northern blot show no significant replicative capacity difference between WT and A372V, ruling out increased replication as resistance cause.
Amiloride treatment increases mutation frequency of wild type CVB3; A372V shows lower basal and induced frequencies.
Poliovirus high-fidelity variant G64S also resists amiloride mutagenesis, confirming generality.
S299T variant, isolated for amiloride resistance, shows lower fidelity in vitro and higher basal mutation frequency, but mutation frequency unchanged by amiloride.
S299T resists both RNA synthesis inhibition and mutagenesis by amiloride, unlike A372V which only resists mutagenesis via higher fidelity.
Dose-response shows amiloride mutagenesis is dose-independent, while RNA synthesis inhibition is dose-dependent, indicating mutagenesis is secondary.
Hypothesize that amiloride mutagenesis is indirect, via changes in intracellular Mg2+ and Mn2+ concentrations.
High extracellular Mg2+ or Mn2+ increase mutation frequency of WT and A372V, but not S299T; passaging in high ions selects for A372V.
Conclude that amiloride-induced mutagenesis results from altered intracellular divalent cation cofactors, with two resistance mechanisms: increased fidelity (A372V) and ion insensitivity (S299T).
Limitations: mutagenic effect moderate, unclear in vivo relevance, unknown direct ion targets, relative contribution of mutagenesis vs. RNA synthesis inhibition not fully quantified.

edges.reason: Screen leads to isolation of A372V variant.
A372V variant characterized for mutation frequency.
In vitro assay confirms higher fidelity suggested by mutation frequency.
Dual resistance observation leads to amiloride mutagenesis hypothesis.
Test alternative explanation (replicative capacity) before accepting hypothesis.
Ruling out replicative capacity allows direct test of mutagenesis hypothesis.
Poliovirus G64S confirms generality of amiloride mutagenesis and fidelity-based resistance.
Hypothesis prompts investigation of other amiloride-resistant variant S299T.
Characterization of S299T reveals distinct resistance mechanism.
Dose-response clarifies primary vs. secondary effects of amiloride.
Dose-independence suggests indirect mechanism, leading to ion hypothesis.
Ion hypothesis tested by manipulating extracellular Mg2+/Mn2+.
Ion experiments support conclusion on mechanism and resistance.

Higher fidelity of A372V shown by mutation frequency and in vitro assay
Hypothesis that amiloride is mutagenic due to dual resistance
Ruling out replicative capacity as cause of resistance
Amiloride increases mutation frequency; A372V shows lower frequencies
S299T has lower fidelity but complete resistance to amiloride mutagenesis
S299T resists both RNA synthesis inhibition and mutagenesis
Dose-response shows mutagenesis is secondary to RNA synthesis inhibition
Hypothesis of indirect mutagenesis via Mg2+/Mn2+
High ions increase mutation frequency and select for A372V
Two resistance mechanisms: increased fidelity (A372V) and ion insensitivity (S299T)
